skill.name: optimize
skill.description: Improve interface performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Makes experiences faster and smoother.
skill.summary: Identify and fix performance issues to create faster, smoother user experiences across loading, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size.
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 37 potential issues across external commands, scripts, and weak cryptography patterns. All findings are false positives from markdown code examples and legitimate technical terms. The skill contains only educational documentation with no executable code. No security risks confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Import Pattern Flagged
medium_findings.description: Line 65 contains a code example showing dynamic import() for React lazy loading. This is a standard performance optimization pattern, not a security risk. The example demonstrates legitimate code splitting: `const HeavyChart = lazy(() => import('./HeavyChart'));`
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern is in markdown code block as documentation, not executable code. Standard React pattern for code splitting.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Misidentified as Shell Commands
low_findings.description: 28 instances of backtick patterns flagged as Ruby/shell execution. These are markdown code fence delimiters (```) surrounding HTML, CSS, and JavaScript examples. No actual shell commands present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear markdown syntax for code documentation. Lines 42-247 contain legitimate code examples in fenced blocks.

low_findings.title: Word 'Critical' Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Lines 3, 6, and 246 contain the word 'Critical' in text contexts (e.g., 'CRITICAL: Measure before and after', 'Critical resources first'). Static scanner incorrectly matched this term as a weak cryptographic algorithm reference.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Term 'Critical' appears in plain English text, not in any cryptographic context. Obvious false positive.

low_findings.title: Performance Tools Misidentified as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Lines 99, 123, 149, 170, and 220 mention legitimate web performance monitoring tools (Chrome DevTools, Performance panel, Lighthouse). Static scanner incorrectly flagged these tool names as reconnaissance patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: References to standard browser development tools. Context is web performance optimization, not system reconnaissance.

content.user_title: Optimize Web Performance for Faster Loading and Smoother Interactions
content.value_statement: Slow interfaces frustrate users and hurt metrics. This skill systematically identifies and fixes performance bottlenecks across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size to create faster, smoother experiences.
actual_capabilities: Analyze current performance using Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID/INP, CLS) and identify bottlenecks
Optimize loading performance through image compression, modern formats (WebP, AVIF), responsive images, and lazy loading
Reduce JavaScript bundle size with code splitting, tree shaking, and dynamic imports
Eliminate layout thrashing and minimize DOM operations for smoother rendering
Implement GPU-accelerated animations using transform and opacity properties
Configure performance monitoring with Lighthouse, Chrome DevTools, and real user metrics
limitations: Cannot measure actual performance metrics without access to your codebase or production environment
Does not automatically apply optimizations - provides guidance and code examples for implementation
Framework-specific optimizations (React, Vue, Angular) require adaptation to your specific codebase
Performance improvements depend on your specific use case and require before/after measurement to verify impact

use_cases.title: E-commerce Site Speed Optimization
use_cases.description: Improve page load times and Core Web Vitals scores for an online store to increase conversion rates and reduce bounce rates. Focus on LCP optimization, image compression, and JavaScript bundle reduction.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developer working on e-commerce performance

use_cases.title: Dashboard Rendering Performance
use_cases.description: Optimize complex data dashboards with charts and tables to eliminate lag during scrolling and filtering. Implement virtual scrolling, memoization, and efficient DOM updates.

use_cases.title: Mobile Web App Optimization
use_cases.description: Create smooth 60fps animations and fast loading for mobile web applications on slower devices and connections. Use GPU acceleration, reduce bundle size, and implement progressive loading strategies.
use_cases.target_user: Mobile web developer optimizing for touch devices

prompt_templates.title: Measure Current Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a Lighthouse audit on my application and identify the top 3 performance bottlenecks affecting Core Web Vitals. Explain which metrics are failing and what's causing the issues.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting performance optimization for a new project

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Image Loading
prompt_templates.prompt: Review all images on my site and convert them to modern formats (WebP, AVIF). Implement responsive images with srcset, add lazy loading for below-fold images, and compress to 80-85% quality. Show me the before/after file sizes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Reducing page weight from large image assets

prompt_templates.title: Fix Layout Thrashing
prompt_templates.prompt: Find code patterns that cause layout thrashing in my JavaScript files. Look for alternating read/write operations to DOM properties like offsetHeight, scrollTop, or getComputedStyle. Refactor to batch reads then batch writes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Investigating janky animations and scrolling performance

prompt_templates.title: Implement Code Splitting
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my JavaScript bundle and identify opportunities for code splitting. Suggest which routes or components should be lazy-loaded with dynamic imports. Set up route-based splitting and provide the import() syntax needed.

output_examples.input: Analyze the performance of my product listing page and suggest optimizations.
output_examples.output:
Based on the code analysis, I've identified 3 critical performance issues:

1. Large hero image (1.2MB uncompressed) - Convert to WebP with srcset for responsive sizes
2. 18 unused CSS files from component libraries - Remove unused styles with PurgeCSS
3. Product cards triggering 47 reflows on scroll - Implement virtual scrolling and batch DOM reads

Implementing these changes should reduce LCP from 4.2s to ~1.8s and improve TBT by 600ms.

output_examples.input: Show me how to implement lazy loading for images.
output_examples.output: Here's the optimized image markup with lazy loading: Key changes: Added loading="lazy" for below-fold images, decoding="async" for parallel processing, srcset for responsive sizes, and explicit dimensions to prevent layout shift.

output_examples.input: Why is my animation lagging at 30fps instead of 60fps?

best_practices: Always measure performance before and after optimizations using Lighthouse, Chrome DevTools Performance panel, or real user monitoring
Optimize based on actual user impact - prioritize fixes that improve Core Web Vitals (LCP < 2.5s, INP < 200ms, CLS < 0.1)
Test on real devices with slow network connections (3G throttling) - desktop development environments don't represent typical user experience
anti_patterns: Premature optimization - making changes without measuring first or optimizing micro-optimizations while ignoring major bottlenecks
Sacrificing accessibility for performance - never remove alt text, skip links, or semantic HTML to save bytes
Overusing will-change property - applying it everywhere creates excessive GPU layers and memory consumption

faq.question: Which performance metric should I optimize first?
faq.answer: Start with the Core Web Vital that's failing most badly. LCP above 2.5s hurts first impressions, INP above 200ms makes interactions feel laggy, and CLS above 0.1 causes jarring layout shifts. Run Lighthouse to see which needs immediate attention.

faq.question: Do I need to optimize images if they load fast on my computer?
faq.answer: Yes, fast development connections mask real-world performance. Test on 3G throttling. Large images are the #1 cause of slow LCP. Use WebP/AVIF formats, compression at 80-85% quality, and responsive srcset so mobile users don't download desktop-sized images.

faq.question: Why does my animation feel janky even at 60fps?
faq.answer: Frame rate isn't everything. Jank comes from inconsistent frame timing. Avoid layout thrashing by batching DOM reads then writes, use requestAnimationFrame for JavaScript animations, and only animate transform and opacity for GPU acceleration. Use Chrome DevTools Performance profiler to spot long tasks blocking frames.

faq.question: Should I use a CDN for performance?
faq.answer: CDNs help by serving content from edge servers closer to users, reducing latency. They're most beneficial for static assets (images, fonts, JavaScript bundles). Combine with other optimizations like compression, caching headers, and HTTP/2 for maximum impact. For small projects, a CDN may be overkill - optimize your code first.

faq.question: How do I reduce JavaScript bundle size?
faq.answer: Start with webpack-bundle-analyzer or similar to identify bloat. Common fixes: Remove unused dependencies, enable tree shaking, implement code splitting with dynamic imports, lazy load routes and non-critical components, and consider if heavy libraries can be replaced with lighter alternatives or native browser APIs.

faq.question: Is server-side rendering (SSR) worth it for performance?
faq.answer: SSR improves LCP by sending rendered HTML faster, but it increases server complexity and Time to First Byte (TTFB). It's most valuable for content-heavy sites, SEO-critical pages, and slow client devices. For highly interactive apps, client-side rendering with proper loading states and code splitting may be sufficient. Measure both approaches with your real users.
